heroOverview: Melena is the passage of black, tarry, foul-smelling stools caused by the digestion of blood that has originated in the upper gastrointestinal tract — most often the esophagus, stomach, or duodenum. The characteristic appearance and odour result from the conversion of haemoglobin to haematin and other pigments by gastric acid, intestinal bacteria, and digestive enzymes as blood travels through the gut. As little as 50-100 ml of blood — roughly one-third of a cup — entering the upper GI tract is sufficient to produce melena. It is the second most recognisable presentation of upper GI bleeding after hematemesis, and the two frequently coexist when bleeding is brisk. Peptic ulcer disease accounts for approximately 35-50% of cases, and the overall in-hospital mortality from the underlying upper GI bleed is 2-10% in low-risk patients, rising sharply in those with cirrhosis or cardiovascular comorbidity.

keyStats.prevalence: Upper GI bleeding — the source of melena — affects 67-80 per 100,000 people per year; melena is present in approximately 70% of UGIB hospitalisations
keyStats.demographics: More common in adults over 60; men are affected roughly twice as often as women
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age of presentation is approximately 65-70 years in Western cohorts
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 300,000 hospital admissions in the United States annually involve upper GI bleeding, with melena being the presenting complaint in the majority

definition: Melena (ICD-10: K92.1) is the evacuation of black, tarry, sticky, and characteristically foul-smelling stools resulting from digested blood that has passed through the gastrointestinal tract from a source in the upper GI tract, generally proximal to the ligament of Treitz. The colour and odour arise from haematin, a breakdown product of haemoglobin after exposure to stomach acid and intestinal bacteria. A minimum of approximately 50-100 ml of blood loss is necessary to produce a single melenic stool, though smaller volumes may produce tarry-looking stool when gut transit is slow. Melena is considered a key symptom of upper GI bleeding (UGIB) and is classified under gastroenterology. It is clinically important to distinguish it from rectal bleeding (hematochezia), which typically appears bright red and signals a lower GI or — in brisk bleeding — upper GI source. Rarely, melena can originate from the small intestine or even the proximal colon if colonic transit is sufficiently slow. The most common causes mirror those of hematemesis — peptic ulcer disease, esophageal varices, Mallory-Weiss tears, and erosive gastritis. Melena without hematemesis can be the only symptom of a significant bleed, particularly when bleeding is intermittent or when a patient does not vomit.

typesClassification.description: The most common pattern; gastric or duodenal ulcers bleed into the gut lumen, producing classic jet-black tarry stools, often with accompanying epigastric pain, though pain may be absent especially in elderly NSAID users.

Portal hypertension causes dilated esophageal or gastric veins that rupture, producing melena often accompanied by hematemesis; high-volume bleeding may produce maroon or frankly red stools despite the upper GI origin.

Slow upper GI bleeding below the volume threshold for visible staining may produce stools that test positive on faecal occult blood testing without any macroscopic colour change; this is often the first sign of gastric cancer or a slowly bleeding ulcer.

Rare cases of bleeding from the small intestine — from vascular malformations, Meckel's diverticulum, or small bowel tumours — can produce melena when transit is slow enough to allow haemoglobin digestion.

primarySymptoms: Black, jet-coloured, tarry stools — the hallmark sign, with a distinctive sticky consistency different from normal dark-coloured stools caused by iron or bismuth.
Foul, penetrating odour unlike ordinary stool, caused by the bacterial degradation of haematin and other blood breakdown products.
Epigastric or upper abdominal pain or discomfort, often preceding the melena, particularly when a peptic ulcer is the cause; pain may be absent in elderly patients or those on NSAIDs.
Nausea and vomiting that may accompany or precede melena, and when vomit contains blood (hematemesis) it confirms an upper GI source.
Lightheadedness or dizziness on standing up, signalling orthostatic hypotension from blood volume depletion.
Fatigue and weakness disproportionate to any other illness, caused by acute anaemia from ongoing blood loss.
Palpitations and a rapid heartbeat as the cardiovascular system compensates for falling circulating blood volume.
Pallor of the skin, lips, and mucous membranes reflecting significant haemoglobin reduction.
Syncope or near-collapse in larger bleeds where circulating volume falls suddenly and cerebral perfusion is transiently insufficient.
Abdominal distension or visible signs of liver disease — spider naevi, jaundice, dilated abdominal veins — when variceal bleeding is the underlying cause.
earlyWarningSigns: A single dark or tarry-looking stool that is unusually sticky and malodorous, even in the absence of any other symptoms.
Unexplained mild fatigue and pallor in someone taking NSAIDs, aspirin, or anticoagulants.
Epigastric discomfort that worsens over several days in a person with a known history of peptic ulcer disease.
Faecal occult blood test returning positive on routine health screening.
emergencySigns: Multiple episodes of tarry black stools within hours, particularly accompanied by dizziness or feeling faint.
Simultaneous vomiting of blood or coffee-ground material alongside passing black stools.
A rapid pulse above 100 beats per minute, cold sweating, pallor, and confusion — signs of haemorrhagic shock.
Systolic blood pressure below 100 mmHg or a blood pressure drop of 20 mmHg or more on standing.
Any melena in a person known to have cirrhosis, portal hypertension, or esophageal varices.

causes.description: Gastric and duodenal ulcers cause roughly 35-50% of all upper GI bleeding episodes producing melena; ulcers erode submucosal vessels, releasing blood that travels distally, is digested, and appears as black tarry stool. NSAID use and H. pylori infection are the dominant triggers.

Portal hypertension from cirrhosis dilates submucosal veins in the esophagus and stomach; when these rupture they release large volumes of blood that may produce both hematemesis and melena simultaneously. Variceal bleeding accounts for 10-20% of UGIB and carries the highest mortality.

NSAIDs, alcohol, aspirin, and physiological stress in critically ill patients cause diffuse superficial mucosal erosions across the gastric or esophageal lining; individually shallow but collectively capable of producing significant chronic blood loss that presents primarily as melena.

Forceful retching tears the mucosa at the gastroesophageal junction; the resulting bleed usually produces hematemesis first, but blood entering the stomach is digested and later appears as melena, typically resolving spontaneously within 24-48 hours.

A persistently large-calibre submucosal artery, usually in the gastric fundus, erodes through a tiny mucosal defect and bleeds massively. It is an uncommon cause (1-2% of UGIB) but produces dramatic hematemesis or melena with little warning.

Upper GI malignancies erode into vessels as tumours enlarge, causing either chronic slow bleeding that presents as melena and iron-deficiency anaemia, or acute major haemorrhage. Cancer is the underlying cause in 1-5% of UGIB admissions.

causes.cause: Angiodysplasia of the stomach or duodenum
causes.description: Ectatic mucosal blood vessels in the stomach or proximal small intestine bleed slowly and intermittently, typically in older adults with cardiovascular disease or aortic stenosis; the bleeding presents more often as melena or occult blood loss than as frank hematemesis.

riskFactors.description: NSAIDs inhibit prostaglandin-mediated mucosal protection, producing gastric erosions and ulcers; regular use increases the risk of significant GI bleeding by approximately 3- to 5-fold, and even low-dose aspirin for cardiovascular protection doubles the risk.

H. pylori disrupts the mucus layer of the stomach and causes gastritis and ulceration; it is present in approximately 70-85% of patients with duodenal ulcers that bleed. Eradication dramatically reduces recurrent bleeding.

Warfarin, direct oral anticoagulants, and combination antiplatelet regimens impair clot formation; a bleeding ulcer that would ordinarily seal spontaneously may instead produce ongoing melena. Risk is highest when the INR is supratherapeutic.

riskFactors.factor: Chronic liver disease and portal hypertension
riskFactors.description: Cirrhosis creates both varices and coagulopathy; patients with cirrhosis have a 30% lifetime risk of variceal bleeding, and each bleed is associated with up to 20% six-week mortality.

Older adults have higher rates of peptic ulcer disease, greater NSAID and anticoagulant use for arthritis and atrial fibrillation, and reduced physiological reserve to compensate for blood loss — all increasing both risk and severity.
Concurrent oral corticosteroids and NSAIDs multiply the GI bleeding risk approximately 15-fold compared with neither drug; corticosteroids alone carry a modest independent risk.
A history of peptic ulcer bleeding without H. pylori eradication or long-term PPI therapy predicts a 30% rebleeding rate within 12 months.
Alcohol directly damages the gastric mucosa, causes erosive gastritis, and in chronic use leads to liver cirrhosis with portal hypertension — all of which predispose to melena.

affectedDemographics: Adults over 60 years, who account for the majority of hospital admissions for upper GI bleeding
Men, at approximately twice the incidence of women for peptic ulcer-related bleeding
Regular users of NSAIDs, aspirin, or anticoagulants — particularly the elderly on multiple medications
People with chronic liver disease, cirrhosis, or established portal hypertension
Critically ill patients in intensive care settings, where stress-related mucosal injury is common
Patients with a personal history of peptic ulcer disease or prior GI bleeding
diagnosisOverview: The diagnosis of melena begins with confirming the character of the stool. Patients and clinicians must distinguish true melena — jet black, tarry, malodorous, sticky — from dark stools caused by iron supplements, bismuth-containing preparations, certain foods (black liquorice, blueberries, beetroot), or charcoal. A faecal occult blood test can confirm the presence of blood when doubt exists. Once melena is established, the assessment mirrors that of any suspected upper GI bleeding. The Glasgow-Blatchford Score is calculated using haemoglobin, blood urea, systolic blood pressure, pulse, and clinical history to stratify risk and guide the need for urgent intervention. Urgent upper GI endoscopy within 24 hours is the definitive investigation, identifying the source in 90-95% of cases and permitting simultaneous haemostasis. Blood tests include a full blood count, coagulation screen, renal function — a raised urea disproportionate to creatinine is a sensitive marker of UGIB — liver function tests, and cross-match for blood grouping. A urea-to-creatinine ratio above 100 (SI units) supports an upper GI source. When the endoscopy is negative but bleeding continues, a CT angiogram or capsule endoscopy can identify small bowel bleeding. Faecal occult blood testing plays a different role in surveillance — it is used in colorectal cancer screening programmes and is not a substitute for endoscopy in acute melena.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the bleeding source in 90-95% of cases and allows simultaneous injection, clipping, or banding to achieve haemostasis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A flexible camera passed through the mouth under sedation; the procedure takes 10-20 minutes; the endoscopist can treat bleeding at the same time.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Stratifies risk of requiring intervention or death at the time of presentation, using only blood tests and clinical data available within minutes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Calculated by the clinical team from blood results and observations taken on arrival; no additional procedure required.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies haemoglobin to measure blood loss; groups and cross-matches blood in preparation for transfusion.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw from the arm; initial haemoglobin may underestimate acute loss before fluid equilibration.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A urea-to-creatinine ratio above 100 (SI) strongly suggests upper GI bleeding because blood is absorbed and metabolised as protein, raising blood urea out of proportion to creatinine.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Part of the standard panel, available within one hour.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects blood in stool when macroscopic colour change is absent or ambiguous; used for confirmation in atypical presentations and for population cancer screening.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small stool sample is collected on a test card and sent to the laboratory; results in 24 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects active bleeding at rates of 0.5 ml/minute or higher when endoscopy is negative or when the patient is too unstable for endoscopy; guides radiological embolisation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A short CT scan after injection of iodinated contrast; takes 5-10 minutes in the radiology department.

treatmentOverview: Management of melena follows the same algorithm as any acute upper GI bleed. Initial resuscitation secures two large-bore intravenous lines, corrects hypotension with crystalloid fluids, and prepares cross-matched blood for transfusion. A restrictive transfusion strategy — targeting haemoglobin at 70-80 g/L rather than liberal transfusion — is now standard of care after evidence showing it reduces mortality by reducing portal pressure in variceal cases and limiting haemodynamic stress. Intravenous proton pump inhibitor therapy — high-dose omeprazole or pantoprazole 80 mg bolus followed by 8 mg/hour infusion — is started empirically in all cases of suspected non-variceal upper GI bleeding to stabilise haemostatic clots and reduce rebleeding. When variceal bleeding is suspected, terlipressin or octreotide is added to lower portal pressure, and prophylactic antibiotics (ceftriaxone 1 g/day for up to 5 days) significantly reduce bacterial complications and improve survival in cirrhosis. Endoscopy within 24 hours (within 12 hours for haemodynamic instability) achieves haemostasis in 85-95% of peptic ulcer bleeds using dual therapy. After haemostasis, confirmed H. pylori eradication and long-term PPI reduce peptic ulcer rebleeding from 30% to under 3%. For variceal bleeding, non-selective beta-blockers combined with repeat endoscopic band ligation form the basis of secondary prophylaxis. Refractory bleeding is managed with transcatheter arterial embolisation or, for varices, a TIPS procedure.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous proton pump inhibitor (PPI)
medicalTreatments.description: High-dose IV omeprazole or pantoprazole given as an 80 mg bolus then 8 mg/hour infusion for 72 hours; suppresses acid to stabilise haemostatic clot over an ulcer, reducing rebleeding risk before and after endoscopy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces rebleeding in peptic ulcer-related UGIB by approximately 40% and need for surgery by 20-30% (Cochrane Review, 2010).

medicalTreatments.name: Terlipressin or octreotide
medicalTreatments.description: Vasoconstrictors that reduce splanchnic blood flow and portal pressure; administered intravenously in all suspected variceal bleeds before and for up to five days after endoscopy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Terlipressin reduces seven-day mortality in variceal bleeding by approximately 34% compared with placebo (Cochrane meta-analysis).

medicalTreatments.name: Prophylactic antibiotics
medicalTreatments.description: Short-course ceftriaxone 1 g/day for up to 5 days in cirrhotic patients with upper GI bleeding; reduces infections including spontaneous bacterial peritonitis and bacterial translocation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces infectious complications by approximately 60% and improves short-term survival (Fernandez et al., NEJM, 2016).

medicalTreatments.name: H. pylori eradication therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Seven to 14-day regimen of two antibiotics plus a PPI (e.g. amoxicillin, clarithromycin, omeprazole) to eliminate the primary driver of peptic ulceration; mandatory after any peptic ulcer bleed.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces peptic ulcer rebleeding from approximately 30% per year to under 3% per year (Cochrane meta-analysis, Ford et al.).

medicalTreatments.name: Restrictive red cell transfusion
medicalTreatments.description: Blood transfusion to maintain haemoglobin between 70-80 g/L; this threshold has been shown to produce better survival than liberal transfusion thresholds in the landmark Villanueva RCT.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Restrictive strategy reduced 45-day mortality by approximately 45% compared with liberal transfusion in one pivotal RCT (Villanueva et al., NEJM, 2013).

medicalTreatments.name: Non-selective beta-blockers for secondary variceal prophylaxis
medicalTreatments.description: Propranolol or carvedilol reduces portal pressure after variceal bleeding is controlled, initiated before hospital discharge in combination with repeat endoscopic band ligation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Combined pharmacological and endoscopic secondary prophylaxis reduces variceal rebleeding from approximately 60-70% to 30-40% at two years.

surgicalOptions.name: Emergency surgical oversewing of bleeding ulcer
surgicalOptions.description: Direct surgical ligation of the bleeding vessel at the ulcer base, typically the gastroduodenal artery; reserved for refractory cases after failed endoscopic and radiological therapy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Achieves haemostasis in approximately 90% of cases but carries operative mortality of 10-20% in the emergency setting, highly dependent on age and comorbidity.

surgicalOptions.name: Transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt (TIPS)
surgicalOptions.description: An interventional radiology procedure connecting portal and hepatic veins via a stent to reduce portal pressure; used when variceal bleeding cannot be controlled by endoscopy and pharmacotherapy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Controls refractory variceal bleeding in 90-95% of cases; early TIPS within 72 hours improves one-year survival in high-risk cirrhotics.

alternativeTreatments.name: Transcatheter arterial embolisation (TAE)
alternativeTreatments.description: Interventional radiology selectively occludes the bleeding artery through a catheter; increasingly used as a bridge or alternative to surgery when endoscopy fails, with lower procedural risk than emergency operation.

alternativeTreatments.name: Iron supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: Not a haemostatic treatment but essential after significant melena; oral or intravenous iron corrects iron-deficiency anaemia over four to eight weeks once the bleeding source is controlled.

whySeeSpecialist: Melena always requires urgent medical evaluation in an emergency or inpatient setting, as the underlying upper GI bleed can escalate rapidly. A gastroenterologist performs the endoscopy that both identifies and treats the source. Patients with portal hypertension need hepatology involvement. Refractory bleeding requires an interventional radiologist or a surgical team. After the acute episode, continued gastroenterology follow-up confirms H. pylori eradication, ensures ulcer healing, and coordinates safe resumption of anticoagulants or NSAIDs where necessary.
doctorSelectionGuide: Acute melena is an emergency managed by emergency physicians and gastroenterologists with 24-hour endoscopy capability. For ongoing management, a gastroenterologist with therapeutic endoscopy experience is ideal, particularly one affiliated with a centre that also offers interventional radiology for embolisation. Patients with known liver disease benefit from combined gastroenterology and hepatology follow-up to manage portal hypertension and secondary prophylaxis.
preventionStrategies: Use the lowest effective dose of NSAIDs for the shortest duration; add a PPI for anyone at increased ulcer risk who requires NSAIDs.
Test for and eradicate H. pylori before starting long-term NSAIDs and after any peptic ulcer bleed.
Avoid or limit alcohol; abstinence is essential for those with cirrhosis and varices.
Manage anticoagulation within therapeutic range and review its necessity periodically.
Screen for varices in newly diagnosed cirrhosis and start primary prophylaxis with non-selective beta-blockers or band ligation.
Attend recommended colorectal and gastric cancer screening to detect precancerous lesions before they bleed.
lifestyleModifications: Avoid NSAIDs and aspirin unless medically necessary; use paracetamol for pain where possible.
Abstain from alcohol, particularly if you have liver disease or a history of GI bleeding.
Stop smoking, which impairs mucosal healing and increases ulcer recurrence.
Take prescribed PPIs consistently and report any return of black stools promptly.
Eat regularly to buffer gastric acid, avoiding prolonged fasting.
Stay hydrated and report any dizziness or pale stools to a clinician promptly.

recommended: Bland, easy-to-digest foods during recovery: plain rice, cooked vegetables, lean protein
Iron-rich foods including lean red meat, legumes, fortified cereals, and leafy greens to restore iron stores after blood loss
Small, frequent meals to dilute gastric acid during the healing phase
Adequate hydration to support circulating volume and kidney function during recovery
avoid: Alcohol in any form, which is directly toxic to the gastric mucosa
NSAIDs and aspirin as self-medication for pain after a GI bleed
Iron supplements, bismuth compounds, and certain foods (blueberries, liquorice) that can cause dark stools and confuse assessment
Spicy or highly acidic foods during the recovery period if they provoke epigastric discomfort

exerciseGuidelines: Complete rest is required during an acute bleed and for the first 24-48 hours after haemostasis is confirmed. A gradual return to light activity over one to two weeks is appropriate as haemoglobin recovers. Strenuous exercise and heavy lifting should be deferred for two weeks after endoscopic treatment of a large ulcer to avoid dislodging haemostatic clips. Regular moderate exercise after full recovery is encouraged, particularly for patients whose weight and alcohol use contribute to their risk.
prognosis: Prognosis depends on the underlying cause, the volume of blood lost, the patient's age, and comorbidities. Overall in-hospital mortality for upper GI bleeding is 2-10% in general populations. Peptic ulcer-related melena has an excellent outcome with prompt treatment: after endoscopic dual haemostasis, rebleeding rates are below 10% in the first 30 days, and long-term recurrence drops to under 3% with H. pylori eradication. Variceal melena carries a 15-20% six-week mortality per episode but improves substantially with secondary prophylaxis. The Rockall Score and Glasgow-Blatchford Score are validated tools for risk stratification. Patients who do not require intervention at initial endoscopy have a very low mortality of approximately 0.1%. Anaemia from melena typically resolves within four to eight weeks with iron supplementation.
recoveryTimeline: After endoscopic haemostasis, patients are observed for 24-72 hours. Those with low-risk lesions can eat and be discharged within 24 hours. High-risk ulcer stigmata require 72-hour observation given concentrated rebleeding risk in the first three days. Oral feeding is restarted within 24 hours in most patients. Iron supplementation corrects anaemia over four to eight weeks. Follow-up endoscopy is recommended for gastric ulcers at 6-8 weeks to confirm healing and exclude malignancy.
complications: Haemorrhagic shock — sustained blood loss depletes circulating volume, causing tissue hypoperfusion, acute kidney injury, and in severe cases multi-organ failure.
Rebleeding — occurs in 10-30% of peptic ulcer cases, concentrated within the first 72 hours; patients with active spurting at endoscopy have the highest rebleeding risk.
Iron-deficiency anaemia — chronic slow melena depletes iron stores before causing visible symptoms; severe anaemia produces fatigue, dyspnoea on exertion, and impaired concentration.
Aspiration pneumonia — vomiting alongside melena in a patient with impaired consciousness can lead to aspiration of gastric contents and chemical pneumonitis.
Missed malignancy — melena from a slowly bleeding gastric cancer may be misattributed to a benign cause if follow-up endoscopy for gastric ulcers is not performed.
Acute kidney injury — renal hypoperfusion from blood loss, compounded by nephrotoxic medications such as NSAIDs, causes acute kidney injury that prolongs hospitalisation.

supportResources.name: NHS — Blood in stools
supportResources.description: UK National Health Service guidance on causes of blood in stools, when to seek emergency care, and what investigations to expect.

supportResources.name: American College of Gastroenterology — Patient Centre
supportResources.description: Patient-facing resources from the ACG on GI bleeding, gastroscopy, and what to expect from endoscopic treatment.

supportResources.name: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases — Upper GI Bleeding
supportResources.description: Detailed patient information on upper GI bleeding causes, risk factors, treatment, and recovery from the US National Institutes of Health.

sources.title: NICE NG141 — Acute upper gastrointestinal bleeding in over 16s: management (2020)
Villanueva C et al. Transfusion Strategies for Acute Upper Gastrointestinal Bleeding. NEJM 2013
Rockall TA et al. Risk assessment after acute upper gastrointestinal haemorrhage. Gut, 1996

confusedWithConditions.name: Hematemesis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hematemesis is blood vomited from the upper GI tract; melena is digested blood passed per rectum. Both come from the same source and frequently coexist, but hematemesis involves vomiting, whereas melena is a stool finding.

confusedWithConditions.name: Colorectal cancer
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Colorectal cancer typically produces fresh red rectal bleeding or faecal occult blood, not black tarry stools; however, proximal colon tumours with slow transit can produce dark stools that may be mistaken for melena, requiring colonoscopy to distinguish.

confusedWithConditions.name: Iron-deficiency anaemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Iron supplements cause dark or black-green stools that can be mistaken for melena; iron deficiency anaemia itself is a consequence of chronic occult bleeding and is distinguished by the absence of macroscopic black tarry stools.

relatedConditions.name: Hematemesis
relatedConditions.relevance: Hematemesis and melena frequently coexist in the same upper GI bleeding episode; together they confirm an upper GI source and together raise the estimated volume of blood loss.

relatedConditions.name: Peptic ulcer disease
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common cause of melena, responsible for 35-50% of acute upper GI bleeding; peptic ulcers from H. pylori or NSAID use erode vessels that bleed into the gut lumen.

relatedConditions.name: Portal hypertension
relatedConditions.relevance: Raised portal pressure causes esophageal varices that rupture and produce melena alongside hematemesis; portal hypertension also impairs coagulation in cirrhosis.

relatedConditions.name: Acute gastric ulcer with hemorrhage
relatedConditions.relevance: A direct and common cause of melena; acute gastric ulcers develop rapidly in hospitalised patients on NSAIDs or aspirin and bleed without prior warning symptoms.

faqs.question: What does melena look like?
faqs.answer: Melena produces stools that are jet black, sticky, and tarry in consistency — very different from normally coloured stool. The smell is distinctively pungent and foul due to bacterial breakdown of digested blood. A single episode requires urgent medical evaluation.

faqs.question: What causes black tarry stools?
faqs.answer: Black tarry stools are caused by blood that has been digested as it travels through the upper gastrointestinal tract. The haemoglobin is broken down by gastric acid and bacteria into haematin, producing the black colour and tarry texture. Common causes include peptic ulcers, esophageal varices, and erosive gastritis.

faqs.question: Is melena always a medical emergency?
faqs.answer: Yes. A single episode of true melena — jet-black, tarry, malodorous stools — is always a medical emergency requiring same-day assessment in hospital. Bleeding can escalate rapidly, and the underlying cause must be identified and treated urgently.

faqs.question: How is melena diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is confirmed by the characteristic appearance and smell of the stool. A faecal occult blood test can confirm blood if doubt exists. The main investigation is an urgent gastroscopy — a camera examination of the stomach — which identifies the bleeding source in 90-95% of cases. Blood tests assess severity and guide transfusion decisions.

faqs.question: Can iron tablets cause black stools like melena?
faqs.answer: Yes. Iron supplements and bismuth compounds can cause very dark or black-green stools that may look like melena. However, iron-related dark stools are formed and not tarry or malodorous, and a faecal occult blood test will be negative unless there is true bleeding. Any doubt warrants medical review.

faqs.question: How much blood is needed to cause melena?
faqs.answer: As little as 50-100 ml of blood entering the upper GI tract — roughly three to six tablespoons — is enough to produce a single melenic stool. Larger volumes produce multiple black stools or a combination of melena and hematemesis.

faqs.question: What is the difference between melena and hematochezia?
faqs.answer: Melena is black tarry stool from digested blood originating in the upper GI tract. Hematochezia is bright red or maroon blood per rectum, typically from a lower GI source such as the colon. Very brisk upper GI bleeding can also produce hematochezia because blood moves too fast to be fully digested.

faqs.question: What tests are needed for black tarry stools?
faqs.answer: You need blood tests — haemoglobin, clotting, kidney function, liver tests, and blood group — as well as an urgent gastroscopy within 24 hours to find and treat the bleeding source. A risk score is calculated from blood results to help decide how urgently endoscopy is needed.

faqs.question: Can aspirin cause melena?
faqs.answer: Yes. Even low-dose aspirin taken for cardiovascular protection damages the stomach lining and can cause peptic ulcers or erosive gastritis that bleed into the gut and produce melena. Anyone taking aspirin who notices black stools should seek same-day medical attention.

faqs.question: How is melena treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment begins with resuscitation — intravenous fluids and blood transfusion as needed. An intravenous PPI is given to reduce stomach acid. An endoscopy is performed within 24 hours to find and stop the bleed using clipping, injection, or banding. H. pylori is tested and treated. Long-term PPI prevents recurrence.

faqs.question: How long does melena last?
faqs.answer: After successful endoscopic haemostasis, stools gradually lighten from black to dark brown over one to three days as residual digested blood clears the gut. Return of black stools after initial improvement is a warning sign of rebleeding that requires immediate re-evaluation.

faqs.question: Is melena the same as blood in the stool?
faqs.answer: Melena is one type of blood in the stool — specifically digested blood from the upper GI tract that appears black and tarry. Bright red blood in the stool (hematochezia) comes from a lower source. Both require medical assessment, but they have different causes and different investigations.

faqs.question: Can stress cause black stools?
faqs.answer: Severe physical stress — critical illness, major surgery, severe burns — can cause stress-related gastric erosions and ulcers that bleed and produce melena. Psychological stress alone does not directly cause black stools. Critically ill patients in intensive care receive PPI prophylaxis to prevent this complication.

faqs.question: What happens if melena is not treated?
faqs.answer: Untreated melena means the underlying bleed is not stopped; blood loss can accumulate to cause anaemia, haemodynamic shock, and organ failure. Even apparently mild melena from a peptic ulcer can suddenly escalate if a vessel re-opens. Prompt hospital evaluation is essential.

faqs.question: Can food cause stools that look like melena?
faqs.answer: Some foods produce very dark stools — blueberries, black liquorice, and certain red wines can darken stools. Iron supplements and bismuth cause very dark stools. These mimic melena by colour but lack the tarry consistency and foul odour. A faecal occult blood test distinguishes food-related dark stools from true melena.

faqs.question: Does melena mean I have cancer?
faqs.answer: Cancer of the stomach or esophagus causes melena in roughly 1-5% of cases. The majority of melena comes from peptic ulcers or other benign causes. Gastroscopy examines the lining directly and takes biopsies if any suspicious lesion is seen, so cancer is confirmed or excluded at the time of the procedure.

faqs.question: When can I go home after being admitted for melena?
faqs.answer: Low-risk patients — those with a clean-based ulcer or a Mallory-Weiss tear that has stopped bleeding — are typically discharged within 24 to 48 hours after being observed and restarting oral food. High-risk findings require 72-hour monitoring. Your discharge depends on stable observations, normal blood tests, and a safe plan for outpatient follow-up.

faqs.question: What follow-up is needed after melena?
faqs.answer: After discharge, a gastroscopy is repeated at 6-8 weeks for any gastric ulcer to confirm healing and exclude cancer. H. pylori eradication must be confirmed by a breath test or stool antigen test. Long-term PPI is continued for high-risk lesions. Your doctor will also review whether NSAIDs or anticoagulants need adjustment.

faqs.question: Can melena occur without any other symptoms?
faqs.answer: Yes. Melena may be the only symptom of significant upper GI bleeding, particularly in older adults on NSAIDs who do not feel ulcer pain. Silent bleeding producing melena is common in this group and is one reason why black stools should never be dismissed as dietary. Medical assessment is required even without pain or vomiting.

faqs.question: Why does melena smell so bad?
faqs.answer: The intense foul odour of melena comes from the bacterial decomposition of haemoglobin and its breakdown products as blood moves through the gut. Sulphur compounds produced by intestinal bacteria acting on haematin and protein create the characteristic penetrating smell that distinguishes melena from dark stools of dietary origin.

metaDescription: Melena is black tarry stool from digested blood in the upper GI tract, caused by peptic ulcers or varices. Learn signs, diagnosis, and urgent treatment.
